original_abstract: This chapter synthesizes multiple lines of evidence to evaluate past, present, and future transformations within the global water cycle. It builds upon and complements the findings presented in Chapters 2, 3, and 4 regarding observed and projected hydrological changes, as well as the discussions in Chapters 10 and 11 focused on regional climate shifts and extreme events. Our assessment encompasses the fundamental physical mechanisms driving these changes, an analysis of observed trends and their primary drivers, future projections alongside their inherent uncertainties, and the risk of abrupt transitions. To provide a comprehensive view, we integrate paleoclimate records, modern observations, reanalyses, and both global and regional model simulations. The results reveal widespread and non-uniform human-induced alterations to the water cycle. While these changes were partially masked during the 20th century by competing climate drivers, they are poised to be increasingly dominated by greenhouse gas forcing on a global scale.
humanized_abstract: This chapter integrates information from many lines of evidence to consider past, present and future changes in the global water cycle. Building on the analysis of past changes and projected future hydrological changes in Chapters 2, 3 and 4, as well as on the discussion of past and future climate regime changes and climate extremes in Chapters 10 and 11, the physical processes underlying these changes are discussed here together with an analysis of the changes and their causes, their projections and their associated uncertainties, and possibilities of abrupt changes in the water cycle. The integration of information from paleoclimatic studies, present day observations, atmospheric reanalyses and global and regional climate models has revealed large changes to the components of the global water cycle that are primarily caused by anthropogenic activities. These changes were sometimes obscured during the 20th century by the natural climate fluctuations that largely controlled the climate signal of the time; these fluctuations will become much less important in the coming years when the anthropogenic forcing is likely to become dominant.
